cwe: CWE-770 - Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
CWE-434 - Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

threat.title: Large File Upload Denial of Service in Express.js and Flask
threat.description: Web applications that accept file uploads without proper size limits, streaming controls, or resource throttling are vulnerable to denial of service attacks. Attackers can upload extremely large files (multiple gigabytes) that exhaust server memory, disk space, or network bandwidth, causing the application to crash or become unresponsive for legitimate users. This is particularly critical in multi-tenant environments where one attacker can impact all users.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies a file upload endpoint without size restrictions (e.g., /api/upload or /upload)
2. Crafts a malicious multipart/form-data request with a 10GB file or empty field names
3. Initiates multiple parallel uploads from distributed IPs to bypass rate limiting
4. Server attempts to buffer entire file in memory before validation, consuming 30-80GB RAM
5. Application runs out of memory and crashes with OOM error, or disk fills completely
6. Legitimate users experience 503 errors and service downtime for 15-60 minutes
prerequisites: File upload endpoint without size limits or with only client-side validation
No streaming upload implementation (entire file loaded into memory)
Insufficient rate limiting or resource quotas per user/IP
No disk space monitoring or alerts configured

cve_references.description: Multer DoS vulnerability where malformed multipart/form-data requests with empty field names crash Node.js applications
Multer unhandled exception during parsing of maliciously crafted multipart/form-data requests causing immediate server termination
Werkzeug resource exhaustion in multipart form parsing where a single 1Gbit/s upload can exhaust 32GB RAM in under 60 seconds

real_world_breaches.impact: Production server crashed with 'Out of memory: Killed process' error due to large file uploads being loaded entirely into memory for validation
real_world_breaches.root_cause: PocketBase reads entire file content into memory before validation, not optimal for large files

real_world_breaches.impact: Golang web application consistently died with OOM error when uploading two 618MB files in parallel
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Memory leak in ParseMultipartForm caused by loading entire file into memory instead of streaming

bug_bounty_reports.summary: File upload endpoint allows 10GB uploads causing memory exhaustion and service crash

security_research.title: Multer DoS Vulnerability: How a Single Malformed Upload Can Crash Your Node.js App
security_research.key_finding: Malformed multipart requests with empty field names trigger unhandled exceptions in Multer, causing immediate process termination

security_research.title: Analyzing CVE-2024-49767: Werkzeug Resource Exhaustion
security_research.key_finding: Werkzeug can allocate 3-8x the upload size in memory, bypassing max_form_memory_size settings

mitigations.title: Implement File Size Limits with Multer and Werkzeug
mitigations.description: Configure strict file size limits at the application level to reject oversized uploads before they consume resources. For Express.js, use Multer's limits configuration. For Flask, use Werkzeug's MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH setting.
iso27001_controls: A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
validation_steps: Test uploading file larger than 10MB (should return 413 error)
Test uploading disallowed file types (should reject)
Monitor memory usage during large file upload attempts

mitigations.title: Stream Large Files Directly to Cloud Storage (Azure Blob/S3)
mitigations.description: Instead of buffering uploads in server memory or disk, stream files directly to cloud object storage. This prevents memory exhaustion and provides scalable, durable storage. Use Azure Blob Storage for Azure environments or AWS S3 for AWS.
nist_csf: PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
validation_steps: Create Azure Blob Storage container or S3 bucket
Configure connection string in environment variables
Test uploading 50MB file and verify it appears in blob storage
Monitor server memory usage (should remain low)
Verify blob URL is accessible and file is intact

mitigations.title: Configure Request Size Limits and Rate Limiting for Flask
mitigations.description: Implement strict request size limits and rate limiting in Flask applications to prevent resource exhaustion. Use Werkzeug's MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH and Flask-Limiter for comprehensive protection.
nist_csf: PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
validation_steps: Configure Redis for rate limit storage
Test uploading file larger than 10MB (should return 413)
Test exceeding rate limit (should return 429 Too Many Requests)
Verify streaming upload handles large files without memory spike

indicators: Sudden spike in upload endpoint requests from single IP or ASN
Multiple requests with Content-Length headers exceeding normal thresholds
Server memory utilization jumping to 90%+ during upload requests
Disk space consumption increasing rapidly (>1GB per minute)
HTTP 413 (Payload Too Large) errors clustered within short time window
Process crashes with OutOfMemoryError or SIGKILL signals
Upload requests with suspiciously large Content-Length values (>1GB)

log_queries.query:
// Detect large file upload abuse patterns
let uploadThreshold = 100000000; // 100MB in bytes
let timeWindow = 1h;
AppServiceHTTPLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(timeWindow)
| where CsUriStem contains "/upload" or CsUriStem contains "/api/upload"
| extend ContentLength = toint(CsBytes)
| where ContentLength > uploadThreshold or ScStatus == 413
| summarize 
    TotalUploads = count(),
    TotalBytes = sum(ContentLength),
    AvgFileSize = avg(ContentLength),
    MaxFileSize = max(ContentLength),
    UniqueUserAgents = dcount(CsUserAgent)
  by ClientIP, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where TotalUploads > 10 or TotalBytes > 1000000000 // 1GB
| order by TotalBytes desc

// Detect file upload DoS attempts
event.category: "web" AND http.request.method: "POST" AND url.path: (*upload* OR *file*)
AND (
  http.request.bytes > 100000000 OR
  http.response.status_code: 413 OR
  http.response.status_code: 500
)
| stats count() as upload_count, sum(http.request.bytes) as total_bytes by source.ip
| where upload_count > 5 OR total_bytes > 500000000

index=web_logs sourcetype=access_combined (uri_path="*/upload*" OR uri_path="*/api/upload*")
| eval content_length_mb=content_length/(1024*1024)
| where content_length_mb > 100 OR status=413 OR status=500
| stats count as upload_attempts, sum(content_length_mb) as total_mb, max(content_length_mb) as max_file_mb by src_ip, _time
| where upload_attempts > 5 OR total_mb > 500
| sort - total_mb

fields @timestamp, @message, httpRequest.clientIp, httpRequest.uri, httpRequest.requestSize
| filter httpRequest.uri like /\/upload/
| filter httpRequest.requestSize > 104857600 or httpRequest.status = 413
| stats count() as requests, sum(httpRequest.requestSize) / 1048576 as totalMB by httpRequest.clientIp
| filter requests > 10 or totalMB > 1000
| sort totalMB desc

tools.name: Prometheus + Grafana
tools.usage: Monitor http_request_size_bytes and process_resident_memory_bytes metrics with alerts for abnormal upload patterns

tools.name: Azure Application Insights
tools.usage: Track request duration, dependency failures, and custom events for upload operations
